journal-title-group.journal-title: The Journal of Spinal Cord Medicine

title-group.article-title: Feasibility and safety of two weeks of blood flow restriction exercise in individuals with spinal cord injury
title-group.alt-title: Jønsson et al. Feasibility and safety of two weeks of blood flow restriction exercise in individuals with spinal cord injury
Jønsson et al. Feasibility and safety of two weeks of blood flow restriction exercise in individuals with spinal cord injury

contrib-group.aff: 1Spinal Cord Injury Center of Western Denmark, Viborg, Denmark
2Department of Clinical Medicine, Health, Aarhus University, Aarhus, Denmark
3Hammel Neurorehabilitation Centre and University Clinic, Hammel, Denmark
4Institute of Sports Science and Clinical Biomechanics, Muscle Physiology and Biomechanics Research Unit, University of Southern Denmark, Odense, Denmark
5Department of Neurology, Aarhus University Hospital, Aarhus, Denmark

author-notes.corresp: Correspondence to: Anette Bach Jønsson, Department of Clinical Medicine, Health, Aarhus University, Palle Juul-Jensens Boulevard 11, DK-8200 Aarhus N, Denmark; Ph: +45 78446152. Email: anbajo@clin.au.dk

event.event-desc: Converted to JATS 1.2 by Nova Techset

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2024 The Author(s). Published with license by Taylor & Francis Group, LLC

license.license-p: This is an Open Access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives License (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/), which permits non-commercial re-use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited, and is not altered, transformed, or built upon in any way. The terms on which this article has been published allow the posting of the Accepted Manuscript in a repository by the author(s) or with their consent.

sec.title: Context
sec.p: Reduced muscle strength and function are common after spinal cord injury (SCI). While low-load blood flow restriction exercise (BFRE) enhances muscle strength in healthy and clinical populations, its safety and feasibility in individuals with SCI remain underexplored.

sec.title: Objectives
sec.p: To investigate the feasibility and safety of low-load BFRE in individuals with incomplete SCI.

sec.title: Study Design
sec.p: Case series.

sec.title: Setting
sec.p: SCI rehabilitation center.

sec.title: Participants
sec.p: Six participants with motor incomplete SCI were enrolled in the study.

sec.title: Intervention
sec.p: A two-week BFRE intervention for the lower limbs was conducted twice weekly at 40% total arterial occlusion pressure, using 30-40% of 1-repetition maximum loads.

sec.title: Outcome Measures
sec.p: Feasibility measures, specifically recruitment and eligibility rates and intervention acceptability, were collected. Blood pressure (BP) responses and specific coagulation markers for deep vein thrombosis (DVT) were assessed as safety measures.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Recruitment and eligibility rates were 2.8% and 6.6% for individuals admitted for first-time rehabilitation (subacute SCI) and 8.3% and 13.9% for 4-week readmission rehabilitation stays (chronic SCI), respectively. Intervention acceptability was high, characterized by 95.8% training adherence and low-to-moderate self-reported pain levels. BP responses and changes in C-reactive protein, Fibrinogen, and D-dimer during the intervention remained within clinically acceptable levels.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: BFRE was feasible in an SCI rehabilitation setting despite a low recruitment rate. BFRE imposed no heightened risk of DVT or severe BP fluctuations in the present case series. Additionally, no severe adverse events occurred, and only mild complaints were reported. More extensive safety considerations warrant larger-scale exploration.

sec.title: Trial registration
sec.p: ClinicalTrials.gov identifier: NCT03690700.

institution-wrap.institution: Graduate School of Health, Denmark Helsefonden

funding-group.funding-statement: This work was supported by Aarhus University, Graduate School of Health, Denmark Helsefonden [grant number 19-B-0146]; Jaschafonden [grant number ID-3514]; and with internal research funding from the Department of Neurology, Regional Hospital Viborg.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Spinal cord injury (SCI) can cause lifelong disability, reduced quality of life and decreased life expectancy (1). Nonetheless, from 1990 to 2019, the incidence, prevalence and years of life lived with disability have increased substantially in SCI (2).

While the disease is complex and multifaceted, one important aspect of rehabilitation is to prevent or minimize physical deconditioning and increase physical function, as reduced physical activity entails an elevated risk of cardiovascular diseases and decreased quality of life (3). To optimize recovery of motor performance, a combination of strength training and task-specific exercises is commonly applied during rehabilitation from SCI (4).

Traditional heavy-load (HL) resistance exercise (RE), typically performed at 60-80% of 1-repetition maximum (1RM), is considered the optimal regimen for enhancing maximal voluntary muscle strength (MVC) and muscle volume in healthy individuals (5). However, increased fatigue, impaired postural balance, and skeletal muscle atrophy are some of the common consequences following SCI, resulting in reduced muscle strength and varying degrees of spasticity (6). These factors can complicate the effects of HL-RE. Consequently, efficacious low-load (LL) exercise modalities are needed to enhance physical rehabilitation efforts in individuals with SCI.

Blood flow restriction exercise (BFRE) involves RE combined with a pneumatic cuff applied proximally on the exercising limb(s). The cuff partially restricts blood circulation to and from the working limb muscle, inducing a hypoxic environment (7). BFR is most commonly performed in the context of LL-RE (30-50% of 1RM) but can also be applied in conjunction with other forms of exercise modalities, such as walking, running or cycling (8). The application of ischemia during the concurrent development of muscle tension appears to increase MVC and evoke hypertrophic adaptations in the trained musculature (9).

Previously, meta-analyses have demonstrated that LL-BFRE is superior to free-flow LL-RE in enhancing muscle strength and volume in healthy individuals (10, 11), and showed comparable effects to HL-RE (12). Additionally, previous studies suggest that combining BFRE with electrical stimulation may significantly increase muscle size in paralyzed muscles compared to using stimulation alone in individuals with SCI (13, 14). The capacity of BFRE to induce muscle hypertrophy and strength gains despite the involvement of low-intensity muscle loadings makes it a potentially valuable exercise modality for SCI rehabilitation. However, the feasibility and safety of BFRE in individuals with SCI remain largely unknown, particularly regarding the tolerability of BFRE, recruitment rates, fluctuations in blood pressure (BP), and the risk of severe adverse events, among other factors. A systematic review by the authors examined the safety and efficacy of BFRE in individuals with neurological disorders and revealed that BFRE appears as a safe training modality for individuals with SCI (15). However, this conclusion is based on a highly limited number of studies with small sample sizes, diverse study designs, and a moderate-to-high risk of bias. Therefore, individuals with SCI may present with an increased risk for adverse events (AEs) during exercise, such as deep vein thrombosis (DVT) or autonomic dysreflexia (AD). Factors such as sensory-motor deficits can pose challenges to the feasibility of BFRE interventions, particularly concerning cuff usage. Due to altered pain perception, individuals with SCI might not notice excessive cuff tightness or potential injuries underneath the cuff. This lack of sensory feedback may increase the risk of complications, such as pressure ulcers, and may also trigger AD episodes or elevate the risk of DVT due to potential excessive blood flow restriction, making BFRE interventions more challenging for this population. Additionally, the recruitment of individuals with SCI for clinical trials has previously been shown to be difficult, and low participation rates are one of the global challenges in SCI research (16). However, to the best of our knowledge, this has not been investigated related to BFRE interventions before. Therefore, further studies are needed to investigate these aspects of BFRE in individuals with SCI.

The aim of the present study was to investigate the feasibility and safety of a short-term LL-BFRE protocol in individuals with SCI. We hypothesized that the relatively conservative BFRE protocol used in this study – characterized by a low occlusion pressure, a fixed number of repetitions, and a training frequency of twice per week – would be both feasible and safe for individuals with SCI.

sec.title: Setting and study population
sec.p: The present study was conducted in a SCI in-patient rehabilitation center in Denmark. The center is located in the western part of Denmark and covers a broad geographical area with a background population of ∼3.2 million individuals. Annually, the center admits an average of 90 individuals for long-term sub-acute rehabilitation following injury and 70 individuals for a 4-week follow-up rehabilitation during chronic SCI.

The present study participants were recruited from May 2020 to June 2021. After obtaining informed consent, each participant's medical records were reviewed for eligibility by a specialist consultant neurologist. Inclusion criteria were: Age ≥ 18 years, exhibit a grade 2–4 on the Medical Research Council (MRC) Scale for Muscle Strength of the knee extensors, classification of grades A, B, C or D on the American Spinal Injury Association (ASIA) Impairment Scale (AIS) with a neurological level of injury below T6, and sufficient cognitive ability to follow instructions. Duration of SCI ≥ 1 month and <1 year (From May 2020 to February 2021). However, due to a poor recruitment rate, the original criteria for the duration of SCI were deemed not optimal, as elaborated in the results. Therefore, the duration of SCI was changed to ≥ 1 year for the rest of the study period (From March 2021 to June 2021). Exclusion criteria were: History of DVT and/or clinical episodes of AD, substance abuse, severe mental illness, uncontrolled hypertension, severe arteriosclerosis, coronary arterial disease, coagulopathy, collagen diseases, and severe neuropathies.

All participants received detailed oral and written information before providing informed, written consent. After inclusion, medical history, neurological level of SCI, and demographic and anthropometric data were obtained through the electronic patient record. The study was approved by the Committee on Health Research Ethics (1-10-72-290-18) and was registered at ClinicalTrials.gov as a part of a larger study (NCT03690700).

sec.title: Feasibility
sec.p: Recruitment flow was reviewed by pre-screening all individuals with SCI admitted at the SCI rehabilitation center during the recruitment period. The purpose was to assess the proportion of admitted patients who would possibly be eligible to participate in the study and who consented to enrolment.

Acceptability of the intervention was systematically monitored during the intervention period and included (i) adherence to the prescribed training regimen, (ii) evaluation of pain responses during training sessions and (iii) monitoring of AEs or general complaints. Adherence to the training protocol was assessed by recording whether participants attended all scheduled training sessions or had any absences. Subjective pain perception was assessed using the short-form McGill pain questionnaire five minutes before the initiation of each training session and immediately after the completion of every exercise.

Hospital staff monitored participants for AEs on an ongoing basis, and study staff collected general complaints before each training session. Additionally, during all training sessions, participants were closely monitored for any adverse physiological responses, such as the development of pressure ulcers, dizziness, severe spasticity, fatigue, and abnormal or neuropathic pain responses.

Following completion of the intervention, a custom-made questionnaire was utilized to assess participants’ satisfaction with the training protocol, including exercise volume, exercise selection, duration of a single training session, as well as whether the training and the occlusion bands caused discomfort. All questions had categorical reply options, as detailed in Table 3.

sec.title: Safety
sec.p: Monitoring for possible DVT formation involved assessing coagulation markers from venous blood samples drawn before (PRE: 30 minutes) and after (0-15 minutes (POST1) and 240 minutes (POST2)) the first and last training sessions. Markers of coagulation (fibrinogen and D-dimer) and inflammation (high-sensitivity C-reactive protein (CRP)) were analyzed at the Department of Biochemistry, Viborg Regional Hospital, Denmark, by an experienced biomedical scientist who was also responsible for retrieving the blood samples. Reference values were derived from collective decision-making for the clinical Region as well as existing literature (17, 18).

Continuous BP was monitored at every training session using a fingertip finometer (Finometer Midi, Finapres Medical Systems B.V., Amsterdam, The Netherlands) placed around the distal interphalangeal joint of the fourth finger on the right hand.

sec.title: Intervention
sec.p: Participants performed 45 min of LL-BFRE (30-40% 1RM) of the lower extremities twice/week for 2 weeks, consisting of 4 sets (30 × 15 × 15 × 15 repetitions, 45-sec pause between sets and 3 minutes pause between exercises) of unilateral seated leg extensions and hamstring leg curls. Resistance load was not adjusted during the study due to the short intervention period. To induce BFR, an 11-cm wide contour-shaped pneumatic occlusion cuff (Delfi Medical Innovations Inc., Canada) was placed proximally on the thigh close to the inguinal fold, using an occlusion pressure corresponding to 40% of seated arterial occlusion pressure (AOP). The computerized Delfi Tourniquet System (Delfi Medical Innovations Inc., Canada) was used to monitor and regulate the cuff pressure during exercise. The cuff remained inflated during all four sets (incl. pauses) while deflated between exercises.

sec.title: Determination of arterial occlusion pressure and resistance load
sec.p: As body position influences arterial occlusion pressure (19), the individual's arterial occlusion pressure (AOP) was recorded at baseline while the participant was seated, and the tourniquet was positioned as described. Using Doppler ultrasound (Siemens ACUSON S2000TM), the pulsation of the A. Tibialis posterior was monitored distally to the medial malleolus. The tourniquet pressure was incrementally increased in series of 5 mmHg, starting from 100 mmHg, and the total AOP was determined when pulsations in A. Tibialis ceased (20, 21). The cuff was immediately deflated after AOP was determined. The pressure of the tourniquet used during training was set to 40% of AOP.

To determine the resistance load during BFRE, a 3RM test of unilateral leg extension and leg curl exercises and an RM calculation formula adjusted for sex and training state (22) were conducted at baseline. The 3RM test, acknowledged for estimating maximal lifting capacity over three repetitions in untrained men and women (23), involved a warm-up of 10 light-weight repetitions, followed by a one-minute rest. Subsequently, participants completed a set of 5 repetitions with an extra 10-20% of the initial weight. The 3RM attempt, with a 20-30% weight increase, typically was achieved in the third trial. All trials were separated by 3- to 5-minute rest periods. In successful trials, 10-20% weight was added, and for unsuccessful attempts, 5-10% was removed. No more than 5 trials, encompassing warm-ups, were allowed for 3RM determination.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: Descriptive statistics of baseline characteristics, pain responses, satisfaction and discomfort with the protocol, coagulation markers and BP responses during the first raining session are presented individually for each participant. Study data were collected and managed using REDCap electronic data capture tools (24) hosted at Aarhus University, Denmark. Figures were created in Microsoft Excel 2019 (Microsoft Corporation, Redmond, WA, USA).

sec.title: Results
sec.p:
Six participants were recruited for the present study. The participants' characteristics are detailed in Table 1. Table 1
Baseline characteristics.
ParticipantAgeSexBMIAISNLIEtiology (T/NT)TSISCIM (0–100)tAOP mmHG (R / L)
A28M20.5ATh8T3 months93175 / 165
B52F27.8DTh10NT3 months95200 / 210
C30M21.8DTh10NT1 month84175 / 165
D22M20.7DTh11NT22 years98175 / 185
E21F20.3DTh12NT21 years95185 / 175
F81M29.8DTh9NT14 years65185 / 200
AIS: American Spinal Injury Association Impairment Scale; BMI: Body Mass Index; L: Left; NLI: Neurological Level of Injury; NT, Non-traumatic; R: Right; SCIM: The Spinal Cord Independence Measure; tAOP: Total Arterial Occlusion Pressure; T, Traumatic; TSI: Time Since Injury

sec.title: Feasibility
sec.p:
The recruitment process is presented in Figure 1. During the period from May 2020 through February 2021, the recruitment rate and eligibility rate remained at 2.8% (3/106) and 6.6% (7/106) for first-time admissions, respectively, recruiting 1 participant every 3 months. This recruitment rate was deemed insufficient to sustain the original inclusion criteria regarding the duration of SCI. Consequently, modifications were implemented, requiring the injury duration to be ≥1 year and, therefore, recruiting exclusively from in-patients of the 4-week rehabilitation stay. The new inclusion criteria were evaluated during the period from March 2021 to June 2021, revealing an increased recruitment rate of 8.3% (3/36) and an eligibility rate of 13.9% (5/36). This adjustment allowed for the recruitment of ∼1 patient per month. No dropouts or withdrawals of consent occurred during the study. Figure 1
Flowchart BP: Blood Pressure; DVT: Deep Vein Thrombosis; MMT: Manual Muscle Test; NLI: Neurological Level of Injury; SCI: Spinal Cord Injury; UVI: Urinary Tract Infection

The participants demonstrated a high acceptability of the intervention protocol, maintaining a 95.8% (23/24) adherence to the training sessions. Participant D missed training session 3 due to post-vaccination illness. Pain responses are detailed in Table 2 and are generally in the low-to-moderate range on the visual analogue scale (VAS). Participant A characterized the pain as a transient throbbing and cramping sensation in the thigh. Participant B reported a previously experienced issue of a burning sensation in the lower back. Participant D exhibited a single short-term incident of cramping and tenderness in the legs before the initiation of training. However, in all cases, the training sessions were completed as planned. Table 2
Pain response on the Visual Analogue Scale (0-100 mm) during BFRE.
ParticipantTraining 1Training 2Training 3Training 4

M3: 5M3: 0M3: 0M3: 0
M4: 0M4: 0M4: 0M4: 0
BB: 0B: 0B: 0B: 28
M1: 0M1: 0M1: 0M1: 0

M4: 0M4: 0M4: 0M4: 0
B: Baseline; M1: Measure 1 after completion of right-sided unilateral leg extension; M2: Measure 2 after completion of right-sided unilateral leg curl; M3: Measure 3 after completion of left-sided unilateral leg extension; M4: Measure 4 after completion of left-sided unilateral leg curl;

No severe AEs were encountered during the present study period. Two participants reported complaints of a mild numbness sensation in the foot while the cuff was inflated, which abated immediately following cuff release. Additionally, one participant reported a high degree of knee extensor muscle fatigue during training session 1 of unilateral leg extension. This feeling resolved within 20 s of cuff deflation during the rest period, and therefore the training session continued as planned.

Results from the satisfaction questionnaire are presented in Table 3. Overall, the results indicate an overall satisfaction with the training protocol. Participant A wanted to increase the training volume to 3–4 sessions per week, and participant B wished to include more overall leg exercises in the training. Table 3
Participant satisfaction and discomfort related to the BFRE protocol.
ParticipantABCDEF
Satisfaction
Amount of exercise (twice a week)Too fewAdequate number of timesAdequate number of timesAdequate number of timesAdequate number of timesAdequate number of times
Exercise selection (focus on thigh muscles)Very satisfiedSatisfiedVery satisfiedVery satisfiedVery satisfiedVery satisfied
Duration of a single training session (approx. 35 min)Very satisfiedVery satisfiedVery satisfiedVery satisfiedVery satisfiedVery satisfied
Discomfort
Training was too hardNot at allTo a lesser degreeNot at allNot at allNot at allNot at all
Training caused painNot at allTo a lesser degreeNot at allNot at allNot at allNot at all
Cuffs caused irritationNot at allNot at allNot at allNot at allNot at allNot at all
Response options; Amount of exercise: (1) Too few, (2) Adequate number of times, (3) Too many, or (4) Don't know. Exercise selection and duration of a single training session: (1) Very satisfied, (2) Satisfied, (3) Neither/nor, 4) Dissatisfied, (5) Very dissatisfied, or (6) Don't know. Discomfort: (1) To a high degree, (2) To some degree, (3) Neither/nor, (4) To a lesser degree, (5) Not at all, or (6) Don't know.

sec.title: Safety
sec.p:
Results from the analysis of coagulation markers are presented in Figure 2. Participants D and F exhibited elevated CRP levels prior to and after the 4th training session. Participant D had received the coronavirus vaccine the day before the third training session, which likely influenced the results of the subsequent blood test. Participant F had elevated CRP values due to a urinary tract infection, which was medically treated upon diagnosis by the physicians. Participants A, B and F showed increased D-dimer levels both at the initial and fourth training sessions. The elevated values in participants A, B and F were deemed clinically acceptable by experienced neurologists. Figure 2
Changes in CRP, Fibrinogen and D-dimer The grey area represents the normal range for each marker, with D-dimer values adjusted according to age-specific cut-offs (17, 18)

To assess cardiovascular stress during BFRE training, systolic and diastolic BP were obtained for each participant, as summarized in Figure 3, which illustrates the fluctuations in BP during the first training session. Throughout all training sessions, no abnormal BP responses were observed. Figure 3
Blood pressure responses recorded during 1st training session The data points are presented at intervals of every 5 min

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Based on the present study data, short-term LL BFRE appears to be a feasible training modality in individuals affected by SCI, characterized by a high intervention acceptability with only mild pain responses and close to full compliance. However, eligibility and recruitment rates were low for both individuals with subacute SCI and chronic SCI. Importantly, no severe AEs occurred during the BFRE intervention period, with only mild complaints being reported. Furthermore, observed reactions in coagulation and inflammation markers, as well as fluctuations in blood pressure, were within a clinically acceptable range.

As previously described, recruitment rates of individuals with SCI for clinical trials pose a challenge in SCI research (16). Anderson et al. (2016) investigated facilitators and barriers to participating in a clinical study among individuals with SCI. The highest-rated barriers identified in the study were (i) possible decline in functionality, (ii) potential side effects, and (iii) potential out-of-pocket expenses (25). These barriers must be considered when designing a clinical study. In the present study, the most significant challenge was the stringent inclusion and exclusion criteria, which resulted in a very low eligibility rate. Despite modifying the inclusion criteria for the duration of SCI at mid-study, the recruitment and eligibility rates remained quite low. Specifically, excluding individuals with SCI levels above TH7 posed a particular challenge in increasing participant numbers. Therefore, for future studies, it is recommended to soften the criteria by performing a risk evaluation based on the most recent safety information, for example, by including participants from ambulatory visits or allowing a higher level of neurological injury. Consequently, including participants with higher-level neurological injuries carries the risk of AD episodes. Therefore, it is crucial for researchers to be updated on practical guidelines for managing these episodes (26). Another way to increase participation is by addressing the barriers identified by Anderson et al. (2016) (25) by providing clear participant information on expected changes in functionality during and after participation, potential side effects and out-of-pocket expenses that could help improve recruitment. It should also be noted that the present study was conducted during the COVID-19 pandemic, which may have impacted recruitment and the number of patient inflow to the SCI unit. Individuals with SCI were advised to exercise extra caution during the pandemic due to their potentially increased vulnerability to severe illness. As a result, potential participants may have been hesitant to participate in non-essential activities, including clinical studies, to minimize their risk of exposure to the virus.

The high acceptability of the present BFRE protocol may be attributed to the relatively low pain responses and generally high compliance observed. A possible explanation for the low pain response could be the low percentage (40%) of AOP and the utilization of a non-failure training protocol. High pain responses to BFRE are often associated with more vigorous BFR protocols, with those employing >80% AOP being linked to higher pain intensities and discomfort compared to protocols using 40% AOP (27). Additionally, non-failure BFRE protocols have been shown to induce less discomfort despite evoking similar muscle adaptations compared to failure protocols (28). However, these results were obtained in healthy individuals and may not be directly applicable to individuals with SCI due to altered function of their sensory system (29). Therefore, the alternated sensory perception might also contribute to the low pain responses observed in the present study. As discussed in the introduction, this is a crucial consideration for researchers, as participants with SCI may not notice excessive occlusion pressure or injuries beneath the cuff, potentially leading to pressure ulcers, AD episodes, or an increased risk of DVT. Despite these concerns, the results from the present study did not indicate that participants experienced abnormal stimuli, as blood pressure results were within normal ranges and no severe adverse events occurred.

DVTs are a well-known complications of SCI. A recent meta-analysis has concluded that the incidence of DVT in patients with acute and chronic SCI was 10.9% (95% CI: 8.7%–13.2%) and 5.3% (95% CI: 2.2%–9.7%), respectively (30). The findings in the present study regarding whether BFRE poses a risk of increased D-dimer and Fibrinogen are consistent with existing literature on the safety of BFRE in individuals with SCI. No signs of increased risks of DVT have been observed after BFRE in individuals with SCI (31, 32). However, both the existing literature and the present study lack statistical power in detecting AEs, for which reason these results must be interpreted with caution.

Recent guidelines regarding the risk of cardiovascular disease define the threshold for maximal exercise-induced systolic blood pressure to be 170 mmHg (33). However, these guidelines are based on aerobic exercise. De Sousa et al. (2014) found increases of up to 200 mmHg in systolic blood pressure during the intensity of 40% of 1RM in double leg press in healthy individuals (34). Haslam et al. (1988) found systolic pressure values (mean ± SD) of 173 ± 6 after unilateral leg press at 40% of 1RM in individuals with coronary artery disease (35). Although the present study demonstrated several systolic blood pressure values above 170 mmHg, these were deemed to remain within the acceptable range and also comparable to the findings of the existing literature (34, 35).

sec.title: Limitations
sec.p: This study inherently possesses several limitations. Only six participants completed the BFRE intervention protocol, which limits the generalization of findings. However, given the variability in age and sex among participants, the results may still offer insights on which future studies may be based.

The first author of this paper, an experienced physiotherapist, performed both assessments, supervised the intervention, and conducted the analysis (unless otherwise specified in the methods section). This could potentially introduce a risk of bias.

The finometer used to derive continuous BP data encountered difficulties in obtaining readings from some of the present participants with cold fingers, as the monitor was positioned on the fourth finger. Notably, measurements from participant G are particularly deficient.

The custom-made questionnaire remains yet to undergo validation. In future studies, it would be more advantageous to use validated instruments to ensure robustness and reliability. Nevertheless, the results from the questionnaire are consistent with the low incidence of pain responses and the high compliance observed during training sessions in the present study.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: In conclusion, short-term BFRE was successfully applied in a cohort of individuals with SCI. The recruitment and eligibility rates observed here, being a single-center study, suggest a need for larger multi-center investigations to achieve conclusions based on more robust data sets. Due to the pioneering nature of this study, the protocol was designed with rather strict inclusion criteria due to safety concerns. While this was an additional limiting factor for recruitment it also limits our ability to deduce the findings onto other subpopulations of SCI individuals, in particular frailer patients with tetraplegia. Therefore, the safety of BFRE in SCI requires further exploration in larger-scale studies. Nevertheless, the present data suggest that BFRE does not pose an elevated risk of DVT or severe BP fluctuations during repeated training sessions in individuals with paraplegia. Further, no severe AEs were observed, while only mild complaints of transitory numbness and fatigue were noted.

sec.title: Disclaimer statements
sec.p: Author contributions: A.B.J., H.K., and K.E.S. designed the study. A.B.J. performed the study and wrote the initial draft of the manuscript. S.K., K.E.S., P.A., H.K., and J.F.N. played important roles in interpreting the results, revising the manuscript, and providing important intellectual content. All authors have reviewed and accepted the final version of the manuscript.
Conflicts of interest: The authors have no conflict of interest to declare
Ethical approval: The study was approved by the Committee on Health Research Ethics (1-10-72-290-18).
